---
title: "Case Selection"
---

## Choose a case

::: {.grid .case-grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6 .reader-card}
### [American Revolution](cases/am-rev/artifacts/qmd/case-overview.qmd)

Revolutionary founding, sacred liberty, collective exception, and war.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6 .reader-card}
### [Napoleon / Napoleonic Wars](cases/napoleon/artifacts/qmd/case-overview.qmd)

Imperial glory, army–nation fusion, and translation-sensitive sacrifice.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6 .reader-card}
### [French Revolution / Jacobin Republic](cases/fr-rev/artifacts/qmd/case-overview.qmd)

Draft case for virtue, terror, public safety, and sacred republican peoplehood
in French source text.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6 .reader-card}
### [Lincoln / American Civil War](cases/lincoln/artifacts/qmd/case-overview.qmd)

Union, covenant, battlefield sacrifice, providence, and reconciliation.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6 .reader-card}
### [Hitler / Nazi Germany](cases/hitler/artifacts/qmd/case-overview.qmd)

Purification fantasy, enemy construction, and exterminatory violence.
:::
:::

## Why these cases

V1 uses leader-centered corpora to compare how sacred objects, enemies,
obligation, and sacrifice are organized in political language. The cases are not
treated as identical historical events. They are selected because each can test
some part of the Law of Sacrifice across war, revolution, empire, civil war, and
genocide.

The initial pilot cases for the primary research question are defined here
rather than in the research-question document so that the question can remain
stable as the workbench expands.

## Selection criteria

Cases should satisfy most of these criteria:

- enough public-domain or open-access material for a balanced core;
- clear connection between political violence and sacred collective objects;
- enough phase coverage to test diachronic development;
- enough register variation to test rhetorical setting;
- enough historical difference to make cross-case comparison meaningful;
- source provenance clear enough for citation and audit.

## Pre-v1 expansion rubric

::: {.callout-note title="Status: expansion criteria"}
These criteria govern the bounded pre-v1 corpus expansion window tracked in
GitHub milestone 7 and issue #174. They should be applied before acquiring new
source text. Candidate ranking and final selection happen in issue #176.
:::

The expansion window may add new cases or selected documents only when they
improve the evidentiary design enough to justify added rights review,
annotation, reliability sampling, and publication-status work. Expansion should
test the framework, not simply add more examples that resemble the existing
cases.

The ranked candidate matrix for this window is recorded in
[Pre-v1 Expansion Candidate Matrix](docs/corpus/pre-v1-expansion-candidate-matrix.md).
Rights and provenance decisions for those candidates are recorded in
[Pre-v1 Expansion Rights and Provenance Review](docs/corpus/pre-v1-expansion-rights-provenance-review.md).

### Case-level rationales

Each proposed new case should have at least one primary rationale and may have
secondary rationales:

| Rationale | Use when the case would... |
|---|---|
| Confirmatory | Test whether a strongly sacrificial or purification-centered corpus produces the expected Koenigsbergian pattern. |
| Limiting | Test a case where political violence is present but sacrificial, body-politic, or enemy-death language may be weak, displaced, or absent. |
| Control | Test a case with mobilization or crisis rhetoric that should not be expected to support the Law strongly. |
| Comparative | Add historical, institutional, religious, ideological, geographic, language, genre, or regime-type contrast not covered by the current four cases. |
| Source-availability | Add a case because rights-cleared, well-provenanced sources make it unusually suitable for reproducible method testing. |

A source-availability rationale is not sufficient by itself. It can break ties
between otherwise valuable candidates, but it should not make a weak
comparative case enter the corpus.

### New-case threshold

A new case should be admitted only when it satisfies all mandatory criteria and
scores well on most value criteria.

Mandatory criteria:

- Stable source base with citable provenance.
- Rights status compatible with the intended storage, publication, or
  metadata-only policy.
- At least one analyzable leader-centered or movement-centered corpus layer.
- Clear relevance to one or more research questions.
- Feasible annotation burden before the expanded-corpus freeze.
- Explicit language and translation policy.
- Expected contribution that is not already supplied by Lincoln, American
  Revolution, Napoleon, or Hitler.

Value criteria:

- Improves variation across war, revolution, empire, civil war, genocide,
  authoritarianism, democracy, colonialism, religion, or anti-colonial struggle.
- Adds a strong positive, limiting, or control test rather than another
  near-duplicate positive case.
- Adds a non-English or non-Western corpus only when translation and source
  controls are strong enough for annotation.
- Adds genre or register contrast, such as speeches, orders, manifestos,
  sermons, proclamations, diaries, or bureaucratic implementation language.
- Clarifies the war/genocide distinction, the enemy-as-bringer-of-death
  mechanism, sacred-object formation, or sacrifice without enemy destruction.
- Supports a balanced core that can be compared without hiding major source
  asymmetries.

### Document-level threshold

Additional documents for existing cases should be admitted only when they fill
a defined gap. A document is necessary when it does at least one of the
following:

- Covers a missing phase, turning point, genre, register, audience, or
  rhetorical occasion.
- Tests a known absence or weak point in the current case analysis.
- Repairs a source-quality, provenance, translation, OCR, or coverage problem.
- Adds negative evidence needed to prevent overclaiming.
- Supports a balanced-core comparison that would otherwise be distorted.

A document should be excluded or deferred when it is merely illustrative,
duplicative, background-only, too rights-risky for the intended use, too
fragmentary for reliable annotation, or likely to expand one case without a
clear comparative reason.

### Ranking fields

Candidate-selection work should record these fields for every proposed case or
document:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Candidate ID | Stable short identifier for discussion and later manifest work. |
| Proposed layer | New case, existing-case balanced core, existing-case extended corpus, or metadata-only context. |
| Primary rationale | Confirmatory, limiting, control, comparative, or source-availability. |
| Research-question link | Which primary or secondary research question the candidate tests. |
| Source base | Candidate editions, repositories, transcripts, scans, or local-only sources. |
| Rights risk | Low, medium, high, or blocked, with storage implications. |
| Language policy | Source language, translation aid, gloss policy, and contested terms. |
| Genre/register value | What rhetorical setting the candidate adds. |
| Annotation cost | Low, medium, high, or prohibitive, considering lexical volume and language. |
| Reliability impact | Whether human or model reliability sampling must expand. |
| Comparative gain | What the project learns that the current four-case corpus cannot test. |
| Exclusion risk | The strongest reason to reject or defer the candidate. |
| Recommendation | Admit, defer, metadata-only, reject, or needs rights review. |

### Expansion stop rules

The expansion window should stop before acquisition or annotation continues
when any of these conditions hold:

- The candidate set no longer changes the comparative design.
- Rights or translation risk would force claims beyond what the project can
  responsibly audit.
- Added annotation volume would delay reliability and publication promotion
  without a corresponding methodological gain.
- A proposed source would require unbounded redownload, source harvesting, or
  a broad reset of already verified corpora.
- The expansion would make balanced-core comparison less transparent rather
  than more informative.

## Comparative design

| Case | ID | V1 corpus model | Rationale |
|---|---:|---|---|
| American Revolution | `am-rev` | Washington-anchored collective exception | Tests sacrifice, founding, sacred liberty, and collective exception in revolutionary war language. |
| Napoleon / Napoleonic Wars | `napoleon` | Napoleon-only individual leader corpus | Tests imperial glory, leader-body extension, army/nation fusion, and translation-sensitive sacrifice language. |
| French Revolution / Jacobin Republic | `fr-rev` | Robespierre-centered Jacobin public corpus | Draft expansion case testing republican virtue, terror, public safety, sacred peoplehood, and source-with-glosses French annotation. |
| Lincoln / American Civil War | `lincoln` | newly selected Lincoln corpus | Tests body-politic healing, covenant, experiment, providence, war sacrifice, and systematic absence. |
| Hitler / Nazi Germany | `hitler` | Hitler-centered corpus with limited Goebbels/Goering support if needed | Tests the war/genocide boundary, purification fantasy, enemy as bringer of death, and exterminatory body-politic logic. |
| Cross-case synthesis | `x-case` | synthetic synthesis workspace | Not a historical case; receives case-level outputs for comparison, validation, and synthesis. |

## Limits

Case selection does not imply moral equivalence, identical causation, or a
single psychological mechanism. Cross-case comparison should identify both
shared symbolic operations and historically specific differences.

The case set remains provisional until source discovery confirms that each case
can support a balanced core and the necessary validation checks.
